---
consultant: Sarah Mitchell
prospect: David Chen
company: TechFlow Solutions
date: 2025-01-15
duration: 28 minutes
---

**[00:00] Sarah Mitchell:** Good afternoon, David! Thank you for taking the time to meet today. I know Q1 is always hectic for operations teams. How's everything going at TechFlow?

**[00:15] David Chen:** Hey Sarah, thanks for the flexibility on timing. Yeah, it's been a busy start to the year. We're trying to streamline a lot of our internal processes.

**[00:28] Sarah Mitchell:** That makes sense. Before we dive in, I wanted to quickly recap — you mentioned in our email exchange that your team has been struggling with call quality visibility. Can you walk me through what that looks like day to day?

**[00:45] David Chen:** Sure. We have about 30 sales reps making calls daily, and right now our managers are manually listening to maybe 5% of calls. We know there are issues — our close rate dropped 15% last quarter — but we can't pinpoint exactly where reps are falling short.

**[01:10] Sarah Mitchell:** A 15% drop is significant. When you say you can't pinpoint the issues, is that because of the volume, or is it more about not having a structured framework for evaluation?

**[01:22] David Chen:** Both, honestly. Our managers use a basic checklist, but it's subjective. One manager might rate a call as "good" while another would flag the same call. And with 30 reps doing 8-10 calls a day, there's no way to manually review everything.

**[01:45] Sarah Mitchell:** So you're dealing with inconsistency in evaluation AND limited coverage. How is that affecting your team's development? Are reps getting regular coaching?

**[02:00] David Chen:** Not really. Monthly one-on-ones happen, but the feedback is vague — "you need to qualify better" without specific examples. The good reps get frustrated because they don't know what they're doing right, and struggling reps don't get targeted help.

**[02:20] Sarah Mitchell:** That's a pattern I hear often. The gap between knowing there's a problem and having actionable data to fix it. What have you tried so far to address this?

**[02:35] David Chen:** We piloted a conversation intelligence tool last year — Gong, actually — but it was expensive and our managers found it overwhelming. Too many features, not enough focus on what matters for our specific sales process.

**[03:00] Sarah Mitchell:** Interesting. So the technology wasn't the issue — it was more about fit and usability for your team's workflow. What would an ideal solution look like for you?

**[03:15] David Chen:** Something that scores calls against our actual sales methodology, not generic metrics. We follow a specific 5-phase call structure, and I need to know if reps are hitting each phase properly. And it has to be simple enough that managers actually use it.

**[03:40] Sarah Mitchell:** That's exactly the problem we built PitchParse to solve. Let me share how it works and you can tell me if it fits. We analyze every call against a customizable scoring framework — 10 KPIs weighted to your priorities. Each call gets a 0-100 score with specific evidence from the transcript.

**[04:05] David Chen:** Every call? How does that scale?

**[04:10] Sarah Mitchell:** It's fully automated. Your reps record calls as usual, the audio gets transcribed and analyzed by AI, and within minutes each call has a detailed scorecard. Managers see a dashboard showing trends across the team — who's improving, who needs help, and exactly which skills to coach on.

**[04:35] David Chen:** And the scoring framework — we can customize it to match our call phases?

**[04:42] Sarah Mitchell:** Absolutely. You'd define your 5 phases with time ranges and goals, set KPI weights based on what matters most — for example, if BANT qualification is critical for your sales cycle, you can weight those KPIs higher. The system evaluates every call against your framework, not a generic one.

**[05:05] David Chen:** That addresses the consistency problem too, right? One standard applied to every call.

**[05:12] Sarah Mitchell:** Exactly. No more subjective checklists. Every call is scored the same way, so when a rep gets an 82 one week and a 74 the next, you know exactly which KPIs shifted and why. The coaching recommendations are specific — "In the discovery phase, you missed probing on pain point quantification at the 8-minute mark."

**[05:40] David Chen:** That level of specificity would be huge for our managers. Right now they're spending hours listening to calls. What kind of time savings are we talking about?

**[05:55] Sarah Mitchell:** Our customers typically reduce manual review time by 80-90%. Instead of listening to random calls, managers review the AI-generated scorecards and focus their coaching on the specific gaps identified. One customer with a similar team size — 35 reps — said their managers went from spending 15 hours a week on call reviews to about 3 hours, but with 100% coverage instead of 5%.

**[06:25] David Chen:** That's compelling. Let me ask about the practical side — how does this integrate with our existing tools? We use Salesforce and Zoom for calls.

**[06:38] Sarah Mitchell:** We integrate with Zoom directly for call recording. Transcripts and analyses link back to the opportunity in Salesforce, so managers can see call quality data right alongside deal progression. No manual upload needed.

**[07:00] David Chen:** Good. Now let's talk numbers. What does this cost?

**[07:05] Sarah Mitchell:** We have two main tiers. The Starter plan at $99 per month covers 50 analyses — that works for smaller teams doing targeted reviews. For your team of 30 reps doing 8-10 calls daily, you'd want the Team plan at $249 per month, which gives you 200 analyses and supports up to 25 users with custom KPI configuration.

**[07:30] David Chen:** 200 analyses for 30 reps doing 240+ calls a day? That math doesn't work.

**[07:38] Sarah Mitchell:** Great point. The 200 is the included quota, but most teams don't analyze every single call. The typical approach is analyzing a meaningful sample — new reps get every call analyzed for their first 30 days, experienced reps get a weekly sample. That pattern usually uses 150-180 analyses per month for a team your size. If you need more volume, we can discuss custom pricing.

**[08:05] David Chen:** That makes sense. We wouldn't need every call. What about the budget side — does this come out of our sales ops budget or training budget?

**[08:18] Sarah Mitchell:** That depends on your org structure. Some customers put it under sales enablement, others under ops. Given that this directly impacts close rates, do you have budget allocated for improving sales performance this year?

**[08:35] David Chen:** We have about $50K allocated for sales tools and enablement in Q1-Q2. We've already spent about $15K of that on CRM customization.

**[08:48] Sarah Mitchell:** So roughly $35K remaining. At $249 per month, PitchParse would be about $3K over that period — well within your budget. And based on the 15% close rate drop you mentioned, even recovering a fraction of that would represent significant revenue. What's your average deal size?

**[09:10] David Chen:** Around $45K annually.

**[09:14] Sarah Mitchell:** So if improving call quality helps your team close even 2-3 additional deals per quarter, that's $90K-$135K in new revenue against a $3K investment. The ROI is pretty clear.

**[09:30] David Chen:** Yeah, the math works. Who else needs to be involved in this decision?

**[09:36] Sarah Mitchell:** Good question. Are you the one who can approve a tool purchase in this range, or does it need sign-off from someone else?

**[09:44] David Chen:** For under $5K I can approve it myself. Anything above that goes to our VP of Sales, Jennifer Torres. This would be in my range.

**[09:55] Sarah Mitchell:** Perfect. And in terms of timeline — you mentioned Q1 is busy. When would you ideally want to have this up and running?

**[10:05] David Chen:** If this is going to impact our Q1 numbers, sooner is better. We have a sales kickoff on February 3rd — it would be great to have this ready by then so we can introduce it to the team.

**[10:20] Sarah Mitchell:** That's about two and a half weeks out. Our typical onboarding takes 3-5 business days — that includes configuring your custom scoring framework, setting up integrations, and training your managers. We could start this week and have you fully live well before February 3rd.

**[10:42] David Chen:** What does onboarding look like specifically?

**[10:48] Sarah Mitchell:** Day 1, we do a kickoff call where we map your sales methodology to our scoring framework — you walk us through your 5 phases and we configure the KPIs and weights. Day 2-3, we set up the Zoom and Salesforce integrations and run a batch of historical calls through the system so you have data to calibrate against. Day 4-5, we train your managers on reading scorecards and using the coaching recommendations. By the end of the week, you're live.

**[11:15] David Chen:** That's thorough. I have one concern — how do reps react to this? I don't want it to feel like Big Brother monitoring.

**[11:25] Sarah Mitchell:** That's an important concern and I'm glad you raised it. The most successful deployments frame this as a coaching tool, not a surveillance tool. The scores are designed to help reps improve, not punish them. We actually recommend sharing individual scorecards with reps — when they see specific, objective feedback like "your question quality scored 4/5 this week, up from 3/5 last week," it becomes motivating rather than threatening. Would you like me to share our customer communication templates for rolling this out to teams?

**[11:55] David Chen:** Yes, that would be helpful. I think if we position it right, our reps will actually want the feedback.

**[12:05] Sarah Mitchell:** Exactly. Top performers especially love it because it validates what they're doing well and gives them specific areas to level up even further.

**[12:15] David Chen:** Alright, I'm interested. What are the next steps?

**[12:20] Sarah Mitchell:** Here's what I'd suggest. First, I'll send you a 14-day trial link today so you can start exploring the platform immediately. Second, let's schedule a 30-minute onboarding kickoff for Thursday — that's when we'll configure your custom scoring framework together. Third, I'll include those team rollout communication templates in the follow-up email. Does Thursday at 2 PM work for the kickoff?

**[12:48] David Chen:** Thursday at 2 PM works. Let me check... yes, I'm free. Let's do it.

**[12:55] Sarah Mitchell:** Excellent. I'll send the calendar invite and trial link within the hour. One last thing — would it be helpful to have one of our customer success managers join the kickoff call? They specialize in helping teams configure their scoring frameworks and can share best practices from similar deployments.

**[13:10] David Chen:** Yeah, that would be great. The more expertise the better.

**[13:15] Sarah Mitchell:** Perfect. So to summarize: you'll get the trial link and rollout templates today, we'll meet Thursday at 2 PM to configure your 5-phase scoring framework with a CS specialist, and the goal is to be fully live before your February 3rd sales kickoff. Does that capture everything?

**[13:35] David Chen:** That covers it. Looking forward to Thursday, Sarah. Thanks for the thorough walkthrough.

**[13:42] Sarah Mitchell:** Thank you, David. I'm excited to help TechFlow close that performance gap. Talk to you Thursday!
